evidence.scientific_clarity: Scatter + OLS line + R²/slope/intercept annotation + highlighted OursModel point all present; axes correctly labelled as log10 parameter count vs validation loss.
evidence.typography: No pdf.fonttype=42 set anywhere in gen_figure.py; PDF confirms Type3 (bitmap) DejaVu Sans fonts — text is not editable or selectable.
evidence.palette: Blue (#4878CF) for models, red (#E84040) for OursModel, dark-gray OLS line — no green/red confusion, but palette is not an explicit CB-safe set (e.g. Okabe-Ito); red-blue distinction is weak for protanopes.
evidence.layout_density: figsize=(6,4.5) with tight_layout; annotation box top-right, OursModel label offset avoids overlap; grid lines subtle; no wasted whitespace observed.
evidence.reviewer_readiness: Caption precisely matches figure content with slope, intercept, R², and OursModel coordinates; legend present; title and axis labels consistent with caption.
evidence.vector_fidelity: PDF is genuine vector (paths/lines are vector) but all text is embedded as Type3 bitmap glyphs — confirmed by 'Subtype /Type3' in PDF; pdf.fonttype=42 was never set.
evidence.file_format: figure.pdf (21 KB), figure.png (63 KB), figure.svg, caption.tex, and gen_figure.py all present and non-trivial.
evidence.caption_quality: Caption states the scientific claim (power-law scaling), gives four specific numbers (slope, intercept, R², OursModel coordinates), and identifies the highlighted point — fully self-contained.

overall_strengths: Complete scientific story: scatter, OLS fit, R² annotation, and OursModel highlight all implemented correctly and match the brief.
Caption is publication-quality with specific numerical claims and coordinates that precisely match the figure.
overall_weaknesses: Missing pdf.fonttype=42 (and svg.fonttype=0) means all text in the PDF/SVG is Type3 bitmap — not selectable, not editable, and will look blurry at high zoom; this is a hard requirement for journal submission.
Default DejaVu Sans font and a non-CB-safe red/blue palette; should use a CB-safe palette (e.g. Okabe-Ito) and set a publication-standard font (e.g. Helvetica or a serif).
